0: <header>The source of performance and excretion data affects the environmental impact of pig rearing estimated by life cycle assessment </header>
A.N.T.R. Monteiro, J.-Y. Dourmad, C.A. Moreira, R.M. Rossi, L.F.M. Ferreira, A.E.R. Costa, and P.C. Pozza

Abstract: The objective of this study was to calculate the environmental impact of raising pigs fed diets with 192, 182, 172, or 162 g kg-1 crude protein (CP) from 15 to 30 kg live weight, using a life cycle assessment (LCA) based on either predicted or observed performance and excretion data. The predicted data were calculated for two animal profiles: one with feed intake and weight gain data being representative of the national Brazilian average (yp-AVG), and the other being representative of the specific experimental farm conditions (yp-spc). Performance and excretion of pigs were either measured (yobs) or predicted (yp) for each CP scenario by using InraPorc® model and LCA impacts were calculated from these data. The and Yobs data of performance, nutrient excretion, and LCA were then compared using a Bayesian paired approach. Whatever the calculation methods, the decrease in dietary CP reduced the environmental impacts per kilogram of weight gain on acidifica- tion potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), and land occupation. Predicted data from yp-SPC did not differ from Yobs data except for nitrogen excretion and AP and EP impacts. Predicted data from yp-AVG differed from Yobs for all criteria except for body weight gain, phosphorus excreted, and cumulative energy demand, mainly because of the lower feed conversion ratio for average data. It was concluded that the use of predicted data calculated from specific farm indicators could better explain the environmental impact of pig farming in Brazil compared with the use of national average indicators.
Key words: Bayesian analysis, excretion, environment, low-protein diet, nitrogen.

Résumé : L'objectif de cette étude était de calculer l'impact environnemental de l'élevage de porcelets recevant des aliments contenant 192, 182, 172 ou 162 g kg-1 de matières azotées totales (CP crude protein ) entre 15 et 30 kg de poids vif, à l'aide de l'une analyse du cycle de vie (LCA << life cycle assessment ) réalisée soit à partir de données prédites ou de données observées. Les données prédites ont été calculées pour deux profils animaux: l'un avec les données de consommation et de gain de poids représentatives de la moyenne nationale (yp-AVG) et l'autre représentatif des conditions expérimentales (yp-spc). Les performances et l'excrétion des porcelets ont été mesurés (yobs) et simulés (yp) pour chaque scénario de CP en utilisant le modèle InraPorc® et l'impact environne- mental a été calculé par ACV à partir de ces données. Les données et Yobs de performance, d'excrétion et de LCA ont été comparées en utilisant une approche bayésienne. Quelle que soit la méthode de calcul, la réduction de la teneur en CP diminuait les impacts acidification (AP << acidification potential eutrophisation (EP - eutrophication potential) et utilisation de surface. Les données prédites par yp-SPC ne différaient pas des celles prédites par Yobs, mis-à-part pour l'azote excrété et les impacts AP et EP. Les données prédites par yp-AVG différaient de celles prédites par Yobs pour toutes les variables mis-à-par pour le gain de poids, le phosphore excrété et l'impact demande en énergie, vraisemblablement en raison du plus faible indice de consommation. Ces résultats indiquent que l'utilisation de données prédites à partir d'indicateurs spécifiques à une exploitation ou à une zone de production permettrait de mieux évaluer l'impact environnemental de l'élevage de porcs au Brésil, par rapport à l'utilisation d'indicateurs nationaux moyens.
Mots-clés : analyse bayésienne, excrétion, environnement, aliment à faible teneur en protéines, azote.

5: Available life cycle assessment (LCA) studies have shown that feed production significantly contributes to the environmental footprint of animal production (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005; Mosnier et al. 2011; Garcia-Launay et al. 2014) and, therefore, is an important element to take into account when considering mitiga- tion options. In this context, dietary crude protein (CP) reduction through industrial amino acid (IAA) use was established as a technique which can change nitrogen (N) excretion (Andretta et al. 2014; Toledo et al. 2014), showing a potential to reduce the environmental impact.

The LCA approach is very comprehensive, using a con- cept based on input/output accounting throughout the product life cycle. As a result, many uncertainties are associated to technical parameters, especially in a live- stock system, due to the diversity of production systems and the wide range of agricultural practices (Basset-Mens and van der Werf 2005). This is the main problem for LCA application in those systems; the lack of references on the diversity of systems and the complexity of inter- actions between parameters (Prudêncio da Silva et al. 2010).

Input data can be obtained through models and (or) software or can be based on technical performance indicators from the agroindustry, which might not be adequate for the diversity in production systems overall. Recent research reported that this is even more impor- tant in Brazil due to differences in land-use patterns, eco- systems, and biodiversity, leading to a growing need for region-specific impact characterization (Novaes et al. 2017). The lack of specific information raises questions about whether predicted data represent what actually happens in the pig system related to nutrient excretion and its subsequent emission, and about whether we can characterize the existing variability.

The aims of this study were (i) to calculate the environ- mental impact of raising pigs from 15 to 30 kg - fed with different CP content - based on LCA methodology and by using predicted performance and excretion data (from specific farm indicators and from national average indicators), and (ii) to compare these results with those obtained by using observed performance and excretion data.

7: The definition of system and subsystem boundaries was the same as presented in Monteiro et al. (2019). The LCA considered the activity of piglet production, from 15 to 30 kg of body weight (BW) (starter phase) in four dif- ferent dietary scenarios. Starter phase was chosen because we assumed that the possible differences between predicted and observed data would be related to methodological issues and not to the production
phase. Impacts were calculated at the farm gate and the functional unit considered was 1 kg of body weight gain (BWG) over the starter phase.
Life cycle inventory (LCI) Feed ingredients and transport specification

Resource use and emissions associated with the pro- duction and delivery of inputs for crop production came from the Ecoinvent database version 3 (SimaPro LCA software version 8.0, PRé Consultants, Amersfoort, Netherlands). Life cycle inventory for grains came from Prudêncio da Silva et al. (2010). Data for non-plant feed components came from Wilfart et al. (2016).
8: Four experimental feeds with different CP contents (192, 182, 172, and 162 g kg-1 were evaluated (Table 1), meeting the requirements of standardized ileal digestible amino acids proposed by Rostagno et al. (2011) by adding L-lysine-HCl, DL-methionine, L-threonine, L-tryptophan, L-valine, and L-isoleucine. Diets were formulated to have the same 2451 kcal of net energy kg) of feed and at least 3230 kcal of metabolizable energy kg` (Rostagno et al. 2011). The feed production process at the feed factory was included in the inventory, considering that it would occur in the pig production region (Garcia-Launay et al. 2014).
9: The environmental impact of raising starter piglets was evaluated in three situations, one by using observed data (yobs) and two others by using model predicted data (yp) of performance and nutrient excretion. For Yobs, pigs' performance and nutrient excretion data were obtained from two trials presented in Monteiro et al. (2019); for pigs' performance and nutrient excretion data were obtained through prediction models cali- brated either (i) by using performance data proposed by Rostagno et al. (2011) representing the national average condition (yp-AVG) for barrows from 15 to 30 kg; or (ii) by using performance data obtained by Monteiro et al. (2019) representing a specific condition (yp-spc) also for barrows from 15 to 30 kg. This approach was chosen to assess the inputs as usually done.

To obtain the predicted data of performance and nutrient excretion, InraPorc® model (van Milgen et al. 2008) was used. The software is based on the transforma- tion of dietary nutrients into body protein and lipid, which are then used to predict performance and excretion. However, to predict these data, a feeding strategy and an animal profile have to be defined. Four feeding strategies corresponding to the diets described above (Table 1) and two animal profiles based on Rostagno et al. (2011; yp-AVG) and Monteiro et al. (2019; yp-spc) were defined.

To calibrate the model, the feed intake was modeled as a gamma function of BW (Vautier et al. 2011), and the protein deposition was modeled by a Gompertz function (van Milgen et al. 2008) for each condition (yp-AVG and

15: Emissions to air during piglet production were estimated step-by-step for ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitro- gen oxide, and methane, as described by Garcia-Launay et al. (2014). The amounts of N, phosphorus (P), copper, zinc, potassium, and organic matter excreted by the pigs were obtained from the simulations. The analysis was based on the CML 2001 method as implemented in SimaPro software (PRé Consultants, Amersfoort,

Netherlands, 2014). Thus, we considered the potential impacts of pig production on global warming potential (GWP, kg CO-eq.), eutrophication potential (EP, g PO4-eq.), acidification potential (AP, SO2-eq.), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE, g 1,4-DCB-eq.), cumulative energy demand (CED, MJ), and land occupation (LO, m² yr).

21: <header>Results </header>
Animal performance and nutrient balance

For average daily feed intake (ADFI), the use of spe- cific performance indicators (yp-spc) provides values, on average, 58 g d-¹ higher than the use of national average parameters (yp-AVG; Table 2). Predicted ADFI from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from the observed data with a mean difference of 44 g d-¹. Conversely, predicted ADFI for -AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data with an average difference of 94 g d-¹ (Table 3).

The reduction in dietary CP provided changes in average daily gain (ADG) smaller than 1%, comparing the 192 g with the 162 g of CP kg diet (Table 2). For this parameter, both predicted data did not differ (P : 0.05) from the observed data, with a mean difference between observed and pre- dicted of 31 g d-¹ (Yobs - yp-AVG) and 15 g d-¹ (Yobs - yp-spc), respectively (Table 3).

There was a reduction in N and P intake and excretion with dietary CP reduction. Predicted N excretion showed average values about 29% higher and 34% higher for -AVG and -SPC, respectively, than Yobs (Table 2). The Bayesian estimates showed that predicted N intake from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas N intake from yp-AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data
(Table 3). For N excreted, both -AVG and yp-SPC differed (P<0.05) from observed data.

The predicted P intake and excretion from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas predicted P intake from -AVG was 0.69 g d-¹ lower (P < 0.05) than the observed value (Table 3). For P excreted, both yp-AVG and yp-SPC did not differ (P < 0.05) from observed data.

Life cycle impacts Global warming potential and land occupation

The average values for GWP did not show important variation between the feeding treatments, with values ranging from 1.70 to 1.71 and from 1.74 to 1.75 kg CO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). Effect of dietary treatment was more marked for LO, with values ranging from 1.81 to 2.00 and from 1.85 to 2.05 m² yr kg- BWG for yp-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated GWP and LO from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data. Conversely, LO calculated from yp-AVG was 0.08 m² yr lower (P < 0.05) than Yobs (Table 3).
Potentials of acidification and eutrophication

The average values of AP ranged from 33.9 to 37.3 and from 35.3 to 38.9 g SO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For EP, values ranged from 10.9 to 12.2 and from 11.3 to 12.6 g PO4-eq. kg) BWG for

-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For these catego- ries, both predicted (yp) AP and EP were higher (P < 0.05) than observed ones (yobs). For AP, the average difference was 4.41 g and 5.93 g SO2-eq. for -AVG and -SPC, respec- tively; whereas for EP, the average difference was 0.90 g and 1.31 g PO4-eq. for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 3).
Cumulative energy demand and terrestrial ecotoxicity

The average values of CED ranged from 17.3 to 19.5 MJ kg` BWG for both -AVG and yp-SPC, whereas for TE, they ranged from 4.46 to 4.84 and from 4.61 to 5.00 g of 1,4-DBC-eq. kg- BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively. The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated CED, for both yp-AVG and yp-SPC, did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data (Table 3). Conversely, for TE, both predic- tions differed from observed data (P < 0.05); values for -AVG and yp-SPC were, on average 0.137 g and 0.289 g 1,4-DCB-eq., respectively, lower than the observed (Table 3).

There was a reduction in N and P intake and excretion with dietary CP reduction. Predicted N excretion showed average values about 29% higher and 34% higher for -AVG and -SPC, respectively, than Yobs (Table 2). The Bayesian estimates showed that predicted N intake from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas N intake from yp-AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data
(Table 3). For N excreted, both -AVG and yp-SPC differed (P<0.05) from observed data.

The predicted P intake and excretion from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas predicted P intake from -AVG was 0.69 g d-¹ lower (P < 0.05) than the observed value (Table 3). For P excreted, both yp-AVG and yp-SPC did not differ (P < 0.05) from observed data.

Table 4. Observed (Yobs) and predicted (yp) environmental impacts, per kilogram of body weight gain, of piglets from 15 to 30 kg, fed diets with different contents in crude protein (CP) and amino acid supplementation.

Note: GWP, global warming potential; AP, acidification potential; EP, eutrophication potential; CED, cumulative energy demand; TE, terrestrial ecotoxicity; LO, land occupation; yp-SPC, predicted by using specific performance data from the farm; -AVG, predicted by using performance data representative of national average.
Life cycle impacts Global warming potential and land occupation

The average values for GWP did not show important variation between the feeding treatments, with values ranging from 1.70 to 1.71 and from 1.74 to 1.75 kg CO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). Effect of dietary treatment was more marked for LO, with values ranging from 1.81 to 2.00 and from 1.85 to 2.05 m² yr kg- BWG for yp-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated GWP and LO from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data. Conversely, LO calculated from yp-AVG was 0.08 m² yr lower (P < 0.05) than Yobs (Table 3).
Potentials of acidification and eutrophication

The average values of AP ranged from 33.9 to 37.3 and from 35.3 to 38.9 g SO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For EP, values ranged from 10.9 to 12.2 and from 11.3 to 12.6 g PO4-eq. kg) BWG for

-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For these catego- ries, both predicted (yp) AP and EP were higher (P < 0.05) than observed ones (yobs). For AP, the average difference was 4.41 g and 5.93 g SO2-eq. for -AVG and -SPC, respec- tively; whereas for EP, the average difference was 0.90 g and 1.31 g PO4-eq. for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 3).
Cumulative energy demand and terrestrial ecotoxicity

The average values of CED ranged from 17.3 to 19.5 MJ kg` BWG for both -AVG and yp-SPC, whereas for TE, they ranged from 4.46 to 4.84 and from 4.61 to 5.00 g of 1,4-DBC-eq. kg- BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively. The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated CED, for both yp-AVG and yp-SPC, did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data (Table 3). Conversely, for TE, both predic- tions differed from observed data (P < 0.05); values for -AVG and yp-SPC were, on average 0.137 g and 0.289 g 1,4-DCB-eq., respectively, lower than the observed (Table 3).

Conversely, predicted ADFI for -AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data with an average difference of 94 g d-¹ (Table 3).

The reduction in dietary CP provided changes in average daily gain (ADG) smaller than 1%, comparing the 192 g with the 162 g of CP kg diet (Table 2). For this parameter, both predicted data did not differ (P : 0.05) from the observed data, with a mean difference between observed and pre- dicted of 31 g d-¹ (Yobs - yp-AVG) and 15 g d-¹ (Yobs - yp-spc), respectively (Table 3).

Life cycle impacts Global warming potential and land occupation

The average values for GWP did not show important variation between the feeding treatments, with values ranging from 1.70 to 1.71 and from 1.74 to 1.75 kg CO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). The average values of AP ranged from 33.9 to 37.3 and from 35.3 to 38.9 g SO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For EP, values ranged from 10.9 to 12.2 and from 11.3 to 12.6 g PO4-eq. kg) BWG for

-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For these catego- ries, both predicted (yp) AP and EP were higher (P < 0.05) than observed ones (yobs). The average values of CED ranged from 17.3 to 19.5 MJ kg` BWG for both -AVG and yp-SPC, whereas for TE, they ranged from 4.46 to 4.84 and from 4.61 to 5.00 g of 1,4-DBC-eq. kg- BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively. Conversely, for TE, both predic- tions differed from observed data (P < 0.05); values for -AVG and yp-SPC were, on average 0.137 g and 0.289 g 1,4-DCB-eq., respectively, lower than the observed (Table 3).

There was a reduction in N and P intake and excretion with dietary CP reduction. Predicted N excretion showed average values about 29% higher and 34% higher for -AVG and -SPC, respectively, than Yobs (Table 2). The Bayesian estimates showed that predicted N intake from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas N intake from yp-AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data
(Table 3). For N excreted, both -AVG and yp-SPC differed (P<0.05) from observed data.

The predicted P intake and excretion from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas predicted P intake from -AVG was 0.69 g d-¹ lower (P < 0.05) than the observed value (Table 3). For P excreted, both yp-AVG and yp-SPC did not differ (P < 0.05) from observed data.

Table 4. Observed (Yobs) and predicted (yp) environmental impacts, per kilogram of body weight gain, of piglets from 15 to 30 kg, fed diets with different contents in crude protein (CP) and amino acid supplementation.

Note: GWP, global warming potential; AP, acidification potential; EP, eutrophication potential; CED, cumulative energy demand; TE, terrestrial ecotoxicity; LO, land occupation; yp-SPC, predicted by using specific performance data from the farm; -AVG, predicted by using performance data representative of national average.
Life cycle impacts Global warming potential and land occupation

The average values for GWP did not show important variation between the feeding treatments, with values ranging from 1.70 to 1.71 and from 1.74 to 1.75 kg CO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). Effect of dietary treatment was more marked for LO, with values ranging from 1.81 to 2.00 and from 1.85 to 2.05 m² yr kg- BWG for yp-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated GWP and LO from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data. Conversely, LO calculated from yp-AVG was 0.08 m² yr lower (P < 0.05) than Yobs (Table 3).
Potentials of acidification and eutrophication

The average values of AP ranged from 33.9 to 37.3 and from 35.3 to 38.9 g SO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For EP, values ranged from 10.9 to 12.2 and from 11.3 to 12.6 g PO4-eq. kg) BWG for

-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For these catego- ries, both predicted (yp) AP and EP were higher (P < 0.05) than observed ones (yobs). For AP, the average difference was 4.41 g and 5.93 g SO2-eq. for -AVG and -SPC, respec- tively; whereas for EP, the average difference was 0.90 g and 1.31 g PO4-eq. for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 3).
Cumulative energy demand and terrestrial ecotoxicity

The average values of CED ranged from 17.3 to 19.5 MJ kg` BWG for both -AVG and yp-SPC, whereas for TE, they ranged from 4.46 to 4.84 and from 4.61 to 5.00 g of 1,4-DBC-eq. kg- BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively. The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated CED, for both yp-AVG and yp-SPC, did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data (Table 3). Conversely, for TE, both predic- tions differed from observed data (P < 0.05); values for -AVG and yp-SPC were, on average 0.137 g and 0.289 g 1,4-DCB-eq., respectively, lower than the observed (Table 3).
31: <header>Results </header>
Animal performance and nutrient balance

For average daily feed intake (ADFI), the use of spe- cific performance indicators (yp-spc) provides values, on average, 58 g d-¹ higher than the use of national average parameters (yp-AVG; Table 2). Predicted ADFI from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from the observed data with a mean difference of 44 g d-¹. Conversely, predicted ADFI for -AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data with an average difference of 94 g d-¹ (Table 3).

The reduction in dietary CP provided changes in average daily gain (ADG) smaller than 1%, comparing the 192 g with the 162 g of CP kg diet (Table 2). For this parameter, both predicted data did not differ (P : 0.05) from the observed data, with a mean difference between observed and pre- dicted of 31 g d-¹ (Yobs - yp-AVG) and 15 g d-¹ (Yobs - yp-spc), respectively (Table 3).

There was a reduction in N and P intake and excretion with dietary CP reduction. Predicted N excretion showed average values about 29% higher and 34% higher for -AVG and -SPC, respectively, than Yobs (Table 2). The Bayesian estimates showed that predicted N intake from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas N intake from yp-AVG differed (P < 0.05) from observed data
(Table 3). For N excreted, both -AVG and yp-SPC differed (P<0.05) from observed data.

The predicted P intake and excretion from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data, whereas predicted P intake from -AVG was 0.69 g d-¹ lower (P < 0.05) than the observed value (Table 3). For P excreted, both yp-AVG and yp-SPC did not differ (P < 0.05) from observed data.

Life cycle impacts Global warming potential and land occupation

The average values for GWP did not show important variation between the feeding treatments, with values ranging from 1.70 to 1.71 and from 1.74 to 1.75 kg CO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). Effect of dietary treatment was more marked for LO, with values ranging from 1.81 to 2.00 and from 1.85 to 2.05 m² yr kg- BWG for yp-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated GWP and LO from yp-SPC did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data. Conversely, LO calculated from yp-AVG was 0.08 m² yr lower (P < 0.05) than Yobs (Table 3).
Potentials of acidification and eutrophication

The average values of AP ranged from 33.9 to 37.3 and from 35.3 to 38.9 g SO2-eq. kg BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For EP, values ranged from 10.9 to 12.2 and from 11.3 to 12.6 g PO4-eq. kg) BWG for

-AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 4). For these catego- ries, both predicted (yp) AP and EP were higher (P < 0.05) than observed ones (yobs). For AP, the average difference was 4.41 g and 5.93 g SO2-eq. for -AVG and -SPC, respec- tively; whereas for EP, the average difference was 0.90 g and 1.31 g PO4-eq. for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively (Table 3).
Cumulative energy demand and terrestrial ecotoxicity

The average values of CED ranged from 17.3 to 19.5 MJ kg` BWG for both -AVG and yp-SPC, whereas for TE, they ranged from 4.46 to 4.84 and from 4.61 to 5.00 g of 1,4-DBC-eq. kg- BWG for -AVG and yp-SPC, respectively. The Bayesian estimates showed that calculated CED, for both yp-AVG and yp-SPC, did not differ (P > 0.05) from observed data (Table 3). Conversely, for TE, both predic- tions differed from observed data (P < 0.05); values for -AVG and yp-SPC were, on average 0.137 g and 0.289 g 1,4-DCB-eq., respectively, lower than the observed (Table 3).
34: The values of feed intake were within the range expected for piglets from 15 to 30 kg of BW. In Brazil, Rostagno et al. (2011) assumed for this phase an average feed intake of 1.26 kg d-¹ and an ADG of 746 g The reduction in N intake and excretion as the dietary CP was reduced (Table 2) corroborates previous studies (Andretta et al. 2014; Toledo et al. 2014). It has been reported that N excretion could be reduced by about 8% for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction (Andretta et al. 2014).

Measured N in manure (yobs) was lower than the pre- dicted N excretion values, both for yp-AVG and yp-SPC (Table 2). This result could be due to the ammonia vola- tilization loss for Yobs. Although manure was collected each day, it may be expected that ammonia emission already occurred before manure sampling.
35: The limited variation in GWP impact according to the dietary CP reduction could be the consequence of LCI, which did not consider the production of protein-rich feed grains from deforested areas. In a study considering different soybean production systems, Prudêncio da Silva et al. (2010) observed that CO2-eq. emission was increased by about 47% when protein sources came from recently deforested areas (2005-2008 period in the legal Amazon rainforest and 2001-2004 period in the Cerrado). In that situation, considering CO2-eq. emis- sions due to the effect of land-use change as a conse- quence of rainforest conversion in crop areas (Prudêncio da Silva et al. 2010), the dietary CP reduction through IAA utilization could be an interesting strategy for reducing the environmental impact under this category (Kebreab et al. 2016; Monteiro et al. 2016).
36: <header>Potentials of acidification, eutrophication, and land occupation </header>

There is a high contribution of N and P to the eutrophication process, and N also contributes to the acidification process by ammonia emissions (Guinée et al. 2002). For this reason, a reduction in AP and EP was observed in our study as the dietary CP was reduced. This result is in line with previous research (Ogino et al. 2013; Garcia-Launay et al. 2014; Kebreab et al. 2016).

For LO category, we observed a reduction of impact when CP was reduced, which was already shown in other studies (Mosnier et al. 2011; Garcia-Launay et al. 2014). This could be due to the reduction of soybean inclusion and its replacement by maize and IAA. Considering the grain production in Brazil, Prudêncio da Silva et al. (2014) reported a LO impact for soybean meal more than twice as high compared with maize, since soya is har- vested once in Brazilian cropland whereas for maize the same land parcel is used twice in the same year (called "safra" and "safrinha" crops), and maize yield ha is

higher than soya yield. Besides that, the use of IAA reduces the LO per kilogram of feed (Monteiro et al. 2019). Because feed production accounts for almost 100% of LO impact of pig production, reducing the impact per kilogram of feed will reduce the LO impact of pig production.

38: As the dietary CP was reduced, an increase in CED was observed (Table 4). According to Kebreab et al. (2016), the production of IAA demands high amounts of non-renewable energy and they suggested that this was an area where the industry could take action to reduce impacts. Conversely, Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) observed a reduction in CED when the incorporation of IAA increased and soybean meal decreased. However, that study was conducted in Europe and 100% of soybean was imported, with a high energy demand at the transport step, which was not the case in the present study.

The high TE impact in low-CP diets (Table 4) was due to the increased incorporation of IAA because TE impact for L-lysine manufacturing was 82% higher than for soy- bean meal production (Garcia-Launay et al. 2014). This seems to indicate that the use of maize-soy-based diets, with minimum IAA supplementation, could provide a reduction in TE impact, considering Brazilian pig pro- duction context, without soybean from deforested areas.
39: <header>Verification by using predicted and observed data </header>

The paired t test indicated that model utilization was efficient to predict performance-related variables, as well as nutrient intake, when the model was calibrated with data from the same farm. In this case, a posteriori estimates showed similarities between observed and yp-SPC for most environmental impact categories (except AP and EP) and animal performance data (except N excretion; Table 4). Conversely, when national average performance indicators were used to calibrate the model (yp-AVG) and to predict the performance and excretion at farm level, almost all parameters (except BWG, P excre- tion, and CED) significantly differed from values calcu- lated from the observed data.

For N excretion and AP and EP impacts, both yp-SPC and -AVG predicted values exceeded the values calculated from the observed data (Tables 2 and 4). This result is mainly due to different methods used to characterize N excretion. For the observed value, N excretion was obtained according to a balance method, with the quan- tification of amount and composition of feces and urine (Monteiro et al. 2019), whereas for yp-SPC and yp-AVG, N excretion was calculated as the difference between N intake and N retained into the body, modeled as a Gompertz function (van Milgen et al. 2008).
The N balance method is known to underestimate N excretion mainly because of ammonia emissions from feces and urine, even when excreta are collected each

day with acidification of urine (Quiniou et al. 1995). Other methods such as the comparative slaughter tech- nique (Quiniou et al. 1995) and, more recently, the use of dual energy X-ray absorptiometry to evaluate the body composition in vivo (Pomar et al. 2014) are considered more accurate but cannot be implemented in the field. The alternative for the determination of N excretion in practice is to use a modeling approach. This approach has been shown to be precise when the animal perfor- mance and feed composition are precisely determined (Rigolot et al. 2010). The lower AP and EP impacts obtained from the observed values could then be the result of an underestimation of N excretion due to uncontrolled ammonia volatilization.

42: The use of model predicted data from specific farm performance indicators to estimate the LCA of pig farm- ing clearly appears as more appropriate than the use of national average indicators. For most environmental impact categories, except for AP and EP, the values calcu- lated using this approach did not differ from the values obtained from observed (measured) data. For AP and EP impacts, this approach also appears to be the most appropriate because the use of measured values of N excretion leads to an underestimation of impacts.

The possibility of applying these models and obtaining accurate results is quite important to diffuse the LCA in the national context, allowing the characteri- zation of different production systems and agricultural practices. Besides that, due to the important regional differences found in Brazil, there is a growing need for farm-specific LCA (Novaes et al. 2017). In this sense, the farm-characterization through specific animal profile could contribute in providing robust results on the envi- ronmental impact of pig farming. As models such as InraPorc® are able to describe nutrient utilization and, consequently, nutrient excretion for a specific condition (van Milgen et al. 2008), the farm-characterization can be done for other regions of pig production.

Regarding the reduction of some environmental impact categories due to IAA inclusion, it can also be applied for other scenarios of pig farming. In our pre- vious publication (Monteiro et al. 2016), the increased IAA addition in growing-finishing diets reduced the impact on AP, EP, and LO whatever the context of pig production (i.e., in Brazil and in France, with soybean from southern Brazil and from central-west Brazil).

44: The predicted results indicated that the dietary CP reduction for piglets from 15 to 30 kg of BW, through the IAA supplementation, reduced the environmental impact for AP, EP, and LO, but not for GWP, considering the context of pig and soybean production in southern Brazil.

The use of predicted performance and excretion data as inputs of LCA, when calibrated with specific farm indicators, could better explain the environmental impact of pig farming in Brazil compared with the use of national average indicators.
